If you just set parameters of the connection or clear them, a mock connection would be fine. Some devices, however, expect a response (e.g. because they call some special functions of the VISAAdapter like get_stb or query_binary_values...). In these cases, a mock adapter won't work.

I agree, that instruments should not be adjusted to make the ProtocolAdapter work.

The problem is, that you are not supposed to use normally the adapter's connection directly, because the `connection` depends on the specific adapter type.
A generic mock connection seems good, because that would care for many use cases. Otherwise we had to add several methods (clear, close, timeout property, get_stb...) individually.
Or we could add timeout as a property to the `Adapter`, which will call its connection depending on the Adapter type (ProtocolAdapter stores it as a variable)

It'd be great to test this set of functionality: prepending a header to the lockfile. 

I don't think you need to necessarily use `RuleRunner` to test the `CreateDigest` and `DigestContents` part: those already have ample tests. More important I think is what goes in between. `RuleRunner` can be tricky also, makes the tests more complex.

I do think the test should ensure the `b"%b\n%b" % (lockfile_metadata(req.hex_digest), lockfile_contents.content)` line works correctly. If you end up not using `RuleRunner`, you'd want your normal Python function to handle that and move it out of the rule.

If you do use `RuleRunner`, it would be helpful to extract a helper rule that only handles the updating of the lockfile after it was generated. But that means new boilerpalte like defining a Request type and result type. I think better is to stick with what you have, but update your normal Python function as proposed and use a normal Pytest test for it. Wdyt?

Just to answer some of your points.

1. Its pretty easy to debug a parameterized test because when there's a test failure the test case and the inputs which caused that test to fail are logged making it easy to track down the error.

2. For modifying separate tests if the config behaves different, I think it would be easier to just update the params in the paramerterized test and the test logic once rarther than having to go back and update multiple test cases.

And I think its quite a common pattern too, its used a bit in this repo see [here](https://github.com/getyoti/yoti-python-sdk/search?p=1&q=parametrize).

However, instead of trying to handle all of that here, simply add a line to the [METADATA_MAP dictionary](https://github.com/regebro/pyroma/blob/606db052ef127e664dd52d59ff57c6828974bfed/pyroma/projectdata.py#L15) mapping `requires_python` to `python_requires` (this project uses the legacy Setuptools-specific keys for historical reasons instead of the standard PEP 621 keys or core metadata fields, as it was around long before PEP 517 or 621 existed), and elide all this code here:

so normally num_returns=len(self.pending) would wait until all pending actors come back, however we also have timeout=self.poll_timeout there ... so effectively the logic here is:

1. we wait self.poll_timeout, if all workers come back with a batch in time, great, and continue.
2. if some workers are slow, and don't come back in poll_timeout, we leave them, and continue, so we don't wait more than poll_timeout interval.
3. if all workers are slow, and nothing comes back in poll_timeout though, we will ignore poll_timeout and keep waiting until at least something comes back, because of the while loop. this may or may not be an intended behavior actually, for off policy cases?

For more complex examples where your module is contained within a folder, it may look like this, ``my_module.main`` or ``my_modules.plugin_hooks``.

I think we have examples of both cases in existing OT APIs, for example in CoAP module.

In `coap.h` we have:
https://github.com/openthread/openthread/blob/e3b971e497bb047d44bc1c9722a81c521354a7f4/include/openthread/coap.h#L907-L915
where we have three callbacks `aHandler`, `aTransmitHook`, and `aReceiveHook` all part of same API and all share same `aContext`. 

And we have "connected" callback which is registered separately and can have its own separate context.
https://github.com/openthread/openthread/blob/e3b971e497bb047d44bc1c9722a81c521354a7f4/include/openthread/coap_secure.h#L329-L340

------------

While creating the signature, it lowers the name of the header as well, so we need to lower the header to be able to match the signature. The corresponding code snippet is here in botocore:
`botocore.auth.SigV4Auth.headers_to_sign` (l.231)
```
def headers_to_sign(self, request):
    """
    Select the headers from the request that need to be included
    in the StringToSign.
    """
    header_map = HTTPHeaders()
    for name, value in request.headers.items():
        lname = name.lower()
        if lname not in SIGNED_HEADERS_BLACKLIST:
            header_map[lname] = value
    if 'host' not in header_map:
        # TODO: We should set the host ourselves, instead of relying on our
        # HTTP client to set it for us.
        header_map['host'] = _host_from_url(request.url)
    return header_map
```

The same goes for the codegen_language field. But the assumption is that the sources that end up captured are in fact sources that "qualify as that type".

> Very often, we will want to allow multiple distinct Sources classes; for example, #9638 allows files(), resources(), and Python targets. Asserting that type(input) == output means that the aggregator rule couldn't ergonomically support multiple disjoint Sources types like it currently does.

Somewhere, some caller needs to identify those three types and independently request them in order to operate on them. One approach might be to allow the caller to specify all of the output types that they could handle... then the callsite would look like:
```
sources_field = await Get[HydratedSources](HydrateSourcesRequest(sources_field, outputs=[Resources, Files, PythonSources]))
if isinstance(sources_field.value, Resources):
  ..
elif isinstance(..):
 ..
else:
 ..
```

> expresses this much more clearly than HydrateSourcesRequest(sources_field, output=Sources)

That would be the default for the field, so you would just specify `HydrateSourcesRequest(sources_field)`.

> What's `$(CWD)`? You do not mean `$(PWD)`?

CWD is make's current working directory (i.e. the one it chdired to and actually ran from, including `-C` indirection and all). This is the safest bet.

PWD is a shell variable, and would in general be made available under that name within make, too. But it is not much use, because it only reflects the CWD at the place `make` was started from (without chdir since then).

> Please note that @di suggested to add validation functionality that behaves like validate().errors, so that it integrates nicely into Warehouse code.

He suggested no such thing. He suggested that something like the warehouse functionality go into a *different* project, [pypa/packaging](https://github.com/pypa/packaging), not this one. I think you may have misunderstood him.

> I felt that throwing an exception by default would make code more clumsy. It's just not beautiful. -- I wouldn't want to miss that possibility in general, though.

This is in general not an example of good design, because there are now two different error handling pathways. Additionally, the Pythonic way to signal an error condition is to raise an exception. It is not clumsy and in fact it leads to a lot of clumsy code in downstream consumers. The reason is that in most cases, the functions calling your function can do nothing about an error except abort early and notify the user, if you raise an exception in error conditions, anyone who isn't going to try to recover from the error can just use your function as if it always works because the exception you raise will bubble up the stack until someone either catches it or the program is aborted and the user is notified of the error. If you return an error code instead, you are relying on your consumers to know that your function can either return an error code of some sort or not and raise an error or a handler.

`augmented_counter` is an atomic variable. You should only touch it once per iteration. Here, you are touching it up to five times. There's no guarantee it will have the same value each time.

You need to touch the value exactly once, e.g.
```
int augmented_rows_completed = augmented_counter.fetch_add(1);
```
Then use only the local copy, `augmented_rows_completed` in this iteration.

If you're unsure how to use `std::atomic` or any other language construct, please ask or read the relevant docs. Don't just guess!

Great point! And thanks for pointing this out because it helps me get it straight in my head. Abstractly, Taskgraph is a dependency graph and an instance of a TaskGraph object traverses that dependency graph executing Tasks as allowed and needed. My intent for `transient_run` is that every time a Task is iterated over in the dependency graph (the equivalent of an instantiation of TaskGraph), that Task be executed. 

The confusing stuff about duplicate Tasks may be me over engineering TaskGraph. Since TaskGraph's abstract model is a graph, it's the equivalent of a set, it doesn't make sense to have duplicate nodes because it's the same node. In practice you'd have a similar confusion -- why indicate that a Task exists twice? So TaskGraph does detect this issue and rather than raise an error it logs a warning and discards the Task. I added this because I was anticipating, and use, cases where I'm not sure if a Task has been added to the graph yet and TaskGraph otherwise has no way of querying its state.

For the case you illustrate, I don't think NgRx will allow that to happen.

You can't do this, for example:
```
state.cardStateMap[pinnedCardId].tableExpanded = false
```

That will silently fail because of the frozen state. The tableExpanded property will continue to be true and neither card will collapse.

NgRx is basically forcing you to do something like this:

```
state.cardStateMap[pinnedCardId] = {
  ...state.cardStateMap[pinnedCardId],
  tableExpanded: false
};
```

That will work around the frozen state AND will decouple the pinned card's state from the original card's state. Pinned card will be collapsed but original card will be expanded.

So to paraphrase, it is okay if both cards reference the same object temporarily because when an attribute changes the object will need to be reconstructed anyway. That makes sense, I guess I was overly worried about that intermediate state.

This is a very general utility that I've used in a variety of contexts before. Either producer or consumer being IO bound is one, but the one that's more interesting for us is producer or consumer being GPU bound. Inside of torch operations, the GIL is released, so the producer can work on getting the next batch ready while the previous batch is being run. If you have this:
```Python
for batch in batches:
    loss = model(batch)
    loss.backward()
    optimizer.step()
```
you can replace it with
```Python
for batch in threaded_generator(batches):
    loss = model(batch)
    loss.backward()
    optimizer.step()
```
and now you can do a fair bit of batch construction work quite late in the process without starving the GPU. That's always handy, but especially so when your dataset is so large it needs to be processed lazily.

The clearest reason that I see to avoid putting product-specific code in `internal` is the increased cognitive load that it would place on future editors of `internal/module.py`. When changing this file, our job is made easier by the fact that we don't have to think about specific products as long as certain guarantees are maintained. With this change, people wanting to change this file would have to understand how IAST fits into that picture in greater detail than they would otherwise. For an internal module that's depended on by multiple products, I think it's worthwhile to be very careful about increasing cognitive load.

Based on my understanding, these have to be parameters of `_SessionBase.__init__()` because it may be called from here (i.e. `Session.__init__()`) and `_RepeatedCapabilities.__getitem__()` (which will actually call it with the final values instead of default values).

We couldn't invoke super's `__init__()` later because that requires calling `self.get_channel_names()` and `self.channel_count` first to get the final `all_channels_in_session`, but calling these method and attribute requires repeated capabilities related properties to be populated first, which is currently done in `_SessionBase.__init__()`, so it kinda creates a circular dependency. If we really want to invoke super's `__init__()` later, we'll need to duplicate the logic in `_SessionBase.__init__()` to `Session.__init__()`, which is not desirable in my opinion.

Hence, I suggest that we retain the current approach (my added comment should have made it clear enough that the initial values passed to the super class are just default values that would be overridden).

Exactly the point of the change.


1/ For std::reference<T>, the original implementation determines operator to call via the equivalent of `cast_op_type = make_type_caster<T>::cast_op<std::decay<T>>` and then invokes `operator cast_op_type&()`. Basically removing the reference and the const before getting the cast op, then adding the reference back in.

The original static_assert ensures that there is such an operator as `cast_op_type&` on `make_type_caster<T>`.


2/ The updated implementation determines the operator to call via the equivalent of `cast_op_type = make_type_caster<T>::cast_op<std::add_reference<T>>` and then invokes `operator cast_op_type()`. 
It adds the reference to the desired type before invoking cast_op, and then uses that operator without rewriting.

The static assert is the equivalent, requiring that the cast operator is one of either `T&` or `const T&`. The new behavior allows cast_op<> to select which operator overload to call instead of manipulating it afterwards, and the static_assert requires that the behavior is either the same as before (T&), or allows it to be const (const T&).

It's good that all the original tests pass with the original code; the fact that the added test fails also indicates that the code works as desired.

Let's insert here the kinds of interactions you can have with a default `_CellBorders` object and the expected values:

```rst
>>> cell_borders = cell.borders
>>> cell_borders
<pptx.shapes.table._CellBorders object at 0x...>
>>> cell_borders.top
None  # ---or whatever---
>>> ... etc.
```

The return value for an "empty" attribute (on `_CellBorders` in this case) is an important design decision which affects subsequent parts of the protocol and the structure of the "read"-side acceptance tests. For example, if a sub-object like `_CellBorderLine` unconditionally appears on an attribute, whether a line or whatever is actually defined or not, that would appear in an acceptance test like:

```cucumber
Given a _CellBorders object as borders
 Then borders.left is a _CellBorderLine object
  And borders.top is a _CellBorderLine object
  And borders.right is a _CellBorderLine object
  And borders.bottom is a _CellBorderLine object
```

We don't need to spell out all four cases like this in the protocol, one example is enough to demonstrate the behavior, but in the acceptance test they'll all be there because each is a separate piece of the code.

If `_CellBorders` has any other interesting read-side behaviors (like other attributes) we should exercise them here as well.

Here is my suggestion for a guideline on how to use/handle `ToString()` methods in core modules

- If the `enum` and `ToString()` method are used within the same module (same file) only or their use is limited to OT core modules, then we don't add any checks (no assert or array length check). Examples of this are things like `Mle::MessageTypeToString` or `Neighbor::StateToString()` ... which are only used within core.

- If the method may be used from a OT public API then we either protect it with `OT_ASSERT()` check in the API or check for array length. I would say we leave the decision for which model to use based the method and its use.
   - For example, `Mle::RoleToString()` we use the array length check, since it also used in `otThreadDeviceRoleToString()` and maybe more importantly from `Settings` where we can read it from non-volatile memory (which may potentially have invalid/corrupted value).
   - For `Joiner::StateToString()` which is used from `otJoinerStateToString()` we use `OT_ASSERT()` in `otJoinerStateToString()` implementation to check the value. This should be fine since we expect the `otJoinerStateToString()` to use `otJoinerState` enum.

I think the difference is we'd just need to set up tests on one of RHEL 6, SL 6 or OL 6 rather than multiple of them (plus any RHEL 6 variants we're forgetting about).

If we can be confident that we can automatically enable SCL on all RHEL 6 based systems reliably without a lot of work, I think that's great. I just worry that it's pretty hard to write and test this due to all the variables here such as [RHEL 6 historically not having subscription-manager](https://github.com/certbot/website/pull/286), [other packages/repositories having to be installed/enabled](https://docs.oracle.com/en/operating-systems/oracle-linux/scl-user/section_e3v_nbl_cr.html), or how to enable SCL being distro specific such as `centos-release-scl`.

Config mode requires `pybind11_` versions. If you write `find_package(pybind11`, then you have to have `pybind11_*` variables like `pybind11_FOUND`. Otherwise, you'd have to have `find_package(PYBIND11`, which we don't provide (and you can't provide both on Windows, even if you wanted to). The old system had `PYBIND11_*` variables for subdirectory mode only - we have standardized on using the Config mode variable names when they overlap rather than having to change this based on which mode you used. This is provided for backward compatibility only.

Internally we were still using `PYBIND11_INCLUDE_DIR` and then setting `pybind11_INCLUDE_DIR` at the end - it made more sense to use the proper variable everywhere and just provide `PYBIND11_INCLUDE_DIR` in subdirectory mode.

`prioritized_replay`: We don't support this anymore, as specification of replay buffers is not explicit.
Meaning that If you want to use a prioritized replay buffer, you have to put a Buffer that allowes PER in the `type` field.

The other prioritization args are allowed, but since R2D2 does not use PER by default, the keys are not used.
And if user specifies a new buffer type, he/she can either specify these args explicitly, or they will not be part of the buffer config, which is not an issue because the values we see here are simply the default values of the paper which are also our default values.

OK I stared at the code some more and this does make sense now. (I thought it mattered only for broken symlinks, but that's not the case.)

We pass a dict here that gets built separately for every directory, since we look for `.gitignore` in every directory, so given a directory structure `root/a/b/c.py`, the dict will contain `root/.gitignore`, `root/a/.gitignore`, `root/a/b/.gitignore` in that order. The `break` happens for the first path where the symlink points outside the directory, so it makes sense to ignore all subsequent gitignores as they definitely won't match either.

Agree that it probably makes more sense to apply gitignore *before* resolving symlinks, but that's something do in a separate PR.

Rather than using `UnionMembership` for `Target` we began to break the dam with `def target_types` as an extension point in `register.py`. But I think that I would still prefer doubling down on union here (and there, perhaps) by:
* applying the `def PluginField` hack to give each subclass a unique type and avoid 1) the new extension point, 2) the new concept.
* giving `@union` an optional target/super class parameter that members should match

The reasoning for this is that there is effectively no end to the types of things that go into unions, and it feels arbitrary to move fields out (just as it did to move targets out). Folks cannot avoid learning about `@unions`: right now it might feel like they can, but only because folks aren't writing v2 plugins. To the extent that they have v1 targets to write bindings for, it's only because they actually wrote v1 plugins, and will need to port them to v2 and learn these concepts. No one will write _only_ a target or _only_ add a field: they will also need to learn about `@rules` and unions in order to actually consume targets/fields... so we're only adding concepts by moving them out.

> ...  this content-encoding is not used by many browsers today ...

Not by Mozilla, eg. One guy on the Web seemed to [want it](http://www.sqaforums.com/forums/archive/index.php/t-26299.html?X-ANT-WAF-Attempt=1), though it's hardly a yt-dl application and it was indeed more than two decades ago.

However as `compress` is in RFC 9110 and easy* to support, I included it.

It's a fairly trivial point since encoding is negotiated and `compress` is arguably a different case from `br` (Myspace vs Instagram). We would never have seen a bug report if some site offered `compress` but not `gzip` or `deflate`. You could surely be absolved if yt-dlp failed to reflect this feature, even given the slicker dependency system.

Separately, I wonder what sites do today if the client says `Accept-Encoding: identity` (so no encoding). Would that provoke/alleviate fingerprinting issues?

\* Considering negotiation, the PR doesn't **advertise** any optional encodings, though, since I forgot to include that change.

I set this up the way other Toil components do logging. I've never done logging the cwltool way before.

Presumably we should use only one of these in this module.

I actually would favor the Toil style as the right one to use here. The [cwltool logger setup](https://github.com/common-workflow-language/cwltool/blob/master/cwltool/loghandler.py) is pretty simple; it hardcodes INFO as the log level, logs everything as coming from "`cwltool`", and sends it to a default stream handler. The Toil style is to have loggers that report what file is doing the logging, and it obeys some options (`--logDebug`, `--logInfo`, etc.) to let the user control the log level.

Since Toil's `cwltoil.py` isn't actually `cwltool`, I think it makes sense for it to not log via `cwltool`'s internal logger. Moreover, since I'm adding some debug-level messages, I need a logger that I can set to debug level from the command line to actually see them, and the Toil logger setup has that, while I don't think cwltool's does.

Should Toil maybe be doing something to cwltool's `_logger` to adopt it and get its log messages to go through the same stream handler as Toil's?

Instead, we need a new fixture that _temporarily_ reverts the order back to normal. It will look something like this

```python
@pytest.fixture
def normal_plugin_order():
    teardown_module()
    yield
    setup_module()
```

This will run the test using the normal plugin order and revert to the module's default state after the test is finished. It should be a fixture because that will take care of most corner cases for us, e.g. if the test crashes with an exception the environment will still be restored correctly.

We have to use that fixture by updating the test's signature `def test_fits_get_reader(normal_plugin_order, tmp_path):`.

I think you mean using like this
```
static NetworkDataTlv *Find(const NetworkDataTlv *aStart, const NetworkDataTlv *aEnd, ...)
```
If so, I don't think we can use this. Note that `const NetworkDataTlv *aStart` means that the pointed object is `const` and not the `aStart` pointer itself (i.e., `NetworkDataTlv` object being pointed to cannot be changed).

This flavor of this method wants to return a non-const `NetworkDataTlv *` (which is found from  the sequence of TLVs we get from `aStart` up to `aEnd`). If the original `aStart` and `aEnd` pointers are specifying the sequence of TLVs as `const NetworkData *` then we cannot safely get and return a non-const pointer from the sequence.

def with_credential_store(function):

            store = CredentialsStore(credentials_path=token_path)

Configuration-handling-wise, what Git does, and what I think makes the most sense, is to just not write configuration options that aren't inherently repository-specific into `.git/config`. That way, you automatically get them from any global configuration files instead, and from defaults if they don't appear in any configuration files.

With that scheme, there's no need to read the parent configuration files if you're just updating the repository settings. It's pretty neat.

For example, you can make `author` a repository setting if you really want to by adding it to `git/config` (skipping the `--global` flag to `git config`), but `git clone` won't write it to `git/config` (or look at it at all).

So, if we want to make the revision a global setting unless specified (which I still think is a bit obscure and weird though), the neat Git-like way would be to just not write the revision into `west/config` unless explicitly specified.

TL;DR: I don't think random seed should be set inside of any action parameters. It should only be set globally.

----------------

So I have a problem with randomSeed at the moment. Suppose I have a loop:

```python
controller = Controller()
for episode in range(int(1e8)):
    controller.step(action="RandomizeMaterials", randomSeed=42)
    # Do something...
```

this has me expecting the materials will change once, and then never change after that because the seed is reset each time? (Here, from C#, you'd set Random.Seed = 42).That is how randomSeed is used in other actions, but this is very unnatural and basically introducing a silent bug, as one would expect calling RandomizeMaterials many times inside of this loop would constantly change the materials differently.I think there should instead be something like:

```python
controller = Controller(randomSeed=42)
for episode in range(int(1e8)):
    controller.step(action="RandomizeMaterials")
```

or

```python
controller = Controller()
controller.randomSeed = 42
for episode in range(int(1e8)):
    controller.step(action="RandomizeMaterials")
```

which is done in other libraries, which sets the random seed for everything up front, and then samples from that random seed later. But that might be beyond the scope of this PR.

I think that something like ```_FileContextManager``` would solve this problem. 
Simplified example:

```python
class _FileContextManager:

    def __init__(self, filepath, flags, start):
        self._filepath = filepath
        self._flags = flags
        self._start = start
        self._fobj = None

    async def __aenter__(self):
        loop = asyncio.get_event_loop()
        self._fobj = loop.run_in_executor(
            None, self._open, self._filepath, self._start
        )
        return self._fobj

    def _open(self, filepath, start):
        fobj = filepath.open(self._flags)
        if start:
            fobj.seek(start)
        return fobj

    async def __aexit__(self, exc_type, exc_val, exc_tb):
        loop = asyncio.get_event_loop()
        await loop.run_in_executor(None, self._fobj.close)
        self._fobj = None
```

To build the Q network, the action network, or the DDPG loss, `DDPGPolicyGraph.__init__` would previously have to call a `self._build_*()` method, which would then pass its arguments & some config variables onto a class that did the actual work of building the network or loss (e.g `QNetwork`/`PolicyNetwork`). I didn't see any point in having both a `self._build_*()` method _and_ a separate function or class to carry out the actual work of that method, so I removed one of those layers of indirection. This also made the code fractionally shorter and easier to debug.

As for the `use_state_preprocessor` thing: previously the code was passing the state through a model from `ModelCatalog` (by default, a two-layer FC net), _then_ passing it through an extra two network layers. That was not at all obvious to me the first time I read the code, and I suspect it was unintentional (you don't need a four-layer network to solve Mujoco problems). I decided to disable the preprocessing thing by default, but also added an option to turn it back on in case somebody wants to work with image-based domains, in which case they'll probably want to preprocess the state with a shallow convnet.

Indeed. (`openmp.omp_get_max_threads()` is not equal to the number of cores, so that one works in this context)

> Let's consider that `num_threads` argument is for advanced users and that we modify `OMP_NUM_THREADS` only if it is set. What do you think?

We don't ever modify `OMP_NUM_THREADS`, we simply account for it if specified. I am not 100% certain, but I think the intended order of preference is/should be something like:

```
> num_threads (rolling_ellipsoid if explicitly specified) 
  > openmp.omp_set_num_threads() (prior in the same process) 
    > OMP_NUM_THREADS (if explicitly specified)
      > omp default (typically equal to cpu_count(), but not always), 
```

We can apparently achieve this effect via `num_threads=0` as default (see: https://github.com/scikit-image/scikit-image/pull/4851#discussion_r477223452).

`select_related` is a Django optimization for making queries run faster.  In some cases, if you don't use it when you are accessing a `ForeignKey` value, then if you reference it (like `job_type.name` then it will have to query the `JobType` model/table in order to get that name.  And it has to do a tiny query for EACH record you do it on.  It can be quite slow...  So with `select_related` it does a single query for ALL the records to get the `ForeignKey` data.  It will still work without it, but be slower.

This is tricky. What I do know is that:
- A single Footnote.read() call will parse as many link reference definitions as it can before stopping.
- The Footnote itself won't store any information; it goes into _root_token.footnotes.
- The swap-in replacement I made for Footnote is a LinkReferenceDefinitionBlock (a block token), which in turn contains LinkReferenceDefinitions (a span token) as children. This was the design option that matched the rest of the codebase most closely, and makes both parsing and rendering most straightforward.
- The second removed line was misleading because Footnote tokens do not have children.

The reason for modifying the comment is mainly the first point here: that it will consume all link reference definitions as it can when parsing. All in all I think the updated description is more accurate, so I'd suggest keeping it. Or maybe rewriting it again to something better. Any suggestions?

In general, I'm not a huge fan of the `_string` suffix or reverse Hungarian notation as a whole: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hungarian_notation

Hungarian notation is sometimes useful! For example: For example: https://www.joelonsoftware.com/2005/05/11/making-wrong-code-look-wrong/

These days, Hungarian notation's benefits can be replicated by `NewType()` in Python and similar tricks in other languages, and pure "this is a string" Hungarian (as opposed to "this int is a height, and not a width") is in general a code smell.

So I'd advise against using the `_string` suffix and instead focusing on the purpose of each variable in the algorithm you are constructing, then picking an appropriate name. Sometimes, `new` is good enough, or the best we can come up with. But frequently, we can do better.

In this case, `other_name_string_renamed_to_new_name_string` is simply `conflictingly_renamed_type_name`.

In fact my knowledge of the specific lock operations on Windows improved significantly since I wrote this comment at the beginning. And it became irrelevant.

I may have thought that an administrator could change the default behavior about exclusive locks on file handlers on a particular reading. But from the further verifications I did, I did not ever find an indication that this could be done in fact.

Finally things are very clear from every documentation I found: Windows kept the behavior that has been designed since MS-DOS. By default, the low level APIs on Windows about file handler give an exclusive lock on the file to the caller. And theses APIs allow to acquire on demand a file handler in share mode, that makes possible to obtain several file handlers to the file from different processes.

Many years ago, in the majority of the situation where a process was opening a file would lead to a exclusive lock, and other processes should wait to take a file handler on an already open file, or an exception would be raised.

From my experience, I can not found now any software that would open a file in exclusive mode. It seems that progressively every software made changes to always open files in share mode. And I can read and write files on Windows from different processes in parallel. 

Python is not an exception here. Calling open, os.open, or os.fdopen never create an exclusive lock on the opened file. So for that matter, it appears that the behavior of Python is consistent between Linux and Windows.

So you need an explicit lock on Windows. And this is done using the specific logic that allows to lock a part of the bytes on a file, through msvcrt. So yes, msvcrt logic is mandatory here to have a real lock.

They are immutable, but their content is still references, meaning if you put a mutable type inside it (e.g. dict) then it would allow you to alter state. So to try and prevent accidentally changing it, we would have to pass in deepcopies of everything manually.

Data Classes would also work, but is only part of Py3.7 and backportable to Py3.6, which unfortunately makes them a non-option.

flush_on_reconnect: bool, optional flag which causes a `flush_all()` command to be sent anytime the memcache client is disconnected and then reconnected to a memcached server. If you have a cluster of memcached servers that use a consistent hash ring (or a similar approach to routing) this can prevent stale reads of old keys when a server in the ring is disconnected and then reconnected. Note, however, that a client with an intermittent connection to a memcached server can cause it to be repeatedly flushed, and the same is true for a cluster of memcached servers. That makes this a VERY dangerous thing to enable on a memcache cluster.

I meant to add a comment that layed out the reasons for the # 1. Resolve, # 2. Build, # 3. Install comments seen below. I can do so if desired, but here it is:

You'd think we might be able to just `pip install` all the requirements, but pexes can be multiplatform / multiinterpreter in which case only a subset of distributions resolved into the PEX should be activated. Sometimes there are platform specific wheels, sometimes python version specific dists (backports being the common case). As such, we need to be able to add each resolved distribution to the `sys.path` individually (PEXEnvironment handles this selective activation at runtime). Since `pip install` only accepts a single location to install all resolved dists, that won't work. This means we need to seperately resolve all distributions, then install each in their own chroot. To do this we use `pip download` for the resolve and download of all needed distributions. Then `pip install` to install each distribution individually. As a complicating factor, some requirements are local; ie `./path/to/setup-py/project`, in which case no wheel or sdist is downloaded even though transitive dependencies of the local project are. As such we insert a `pip wheel` step to build wheels from any local project requirements found.

The function `from_html` uses the decorator `@classmethod`. I believe that `@staticmethod` and `@classmethod` should not be mixed in the same file if there isn't a good reason for it. Mixing the two decorators can cause unnecessary confusion for the readers.

Runtime and memory concerns, primarily. We don't want to copy for no reason, especially because these AST objects tend to have a lot of children, whose children have a lot of children etc. We'd just be generating garbage for the GC to collect, even though we can easily avoid it.

Most ASTs don't need to be mutated, so we don't want to pay the copying cost if we don't have to. This approach buys us immutability while still being friendly to the GC.

One big change here is that this is no longer trying to do any caching of collection fetching behaviour - instead relying on browser cache to prevent API requests hitting the server in cases where the cache headers are set.

The only thing this really does is to make sure if we do `fetchModel` after a `fetchCollection` that contained that specific model, we don't do another request to the server just to get that one model that we already have all the data for.

So, yeah, in many ways the cache is doing less - and I think it's appropriate for it to do so, especially when combined with good caching headers on the backend.

* et c'est un barème harmonisé avec les [barèmes IPP](https://www.ipp.eu/baremes-ipp/prelevements-sociaux/1/cotisations_securite_sociale_ss_regime_general/mmid) (cf. taux réduit et normal au seuil de 2.5 SMIC).

`dimension_optiontype` is a thing like `purelist_isregular`, `purelist_depth`, `minmax_depth`, etc. They should be implemented directly as properties on Forms:

https://github.com/scikit-hep/awkward-1.0/blob/8aa771b064f6d6b51872390cc2288aed8b952ed8/src/awkward/_v2/forms/listform.py#L144-L173

and then they can be added to all Content classes without code duplication and without creating short-lived Form objects through a Python trick:

https://github.com/scikit-hep/awkward-1.0/blob/8aa771b064f6d6b51872390cc2288aed8b952ed8/src/awkward/_v2/contents/content.py#L746-L764

The "`.__get__(self)`" is to call a Form property using the Content as `self`. This requires duck typing: the implementation of `dimension_optiontype` must work whether `self` is a Form instance or `self` is a Content instance, but for these simple methods, that's usually not hard to do. For instance, the C++ for IndexedForm and IndexedOptionForm are:

https://github.com/scikit-hep/awkward-1.0/blob/8aa771b064f6d6b51872390cc2288aed8b952ed8/src/libawkward/array/IndexedArray.cpp#L142-L145

https://github.com/scikit-hep/awkward-1.0/blob/8aa771b064f6d6b51872390cc2288aed8b952ed8/src/libawkward/array/IndexedArray.cpp#L388-L391

Forms and Contents consistently use `content`/`contents` as a pointer to their child/children, so that should help with the duck typing. For this, there is no reason to ask `isinstance`.

(Now that the mid-layer is being translated into Python, some of the `isinstance` switch statements in the high-level code will be convertible into Content methods, something that I think we'll be slowly doing as post-v2 cleanup.)

Oh boy. So. How did we get to this point in the execution?
1. We came from run and have a perfectly good RenewableCert that gave us a certname.
2. We came from install and the user specified the certname
3. We came from install and the user specified the paths, but they're our normal default paths and the certname will be right there in the file path
4. We came from install but the user just wants to use our installer functionality separate from everything else we do and just gave us paths to files located who-knows-where.

We currently cover cases 1 and 2. We could easily cover case 3 using `cert_manager.cert_path_to_lineage()`, and if we get an error we're probably just in case 4 and can continue as planned.

Previously we only executed this calculation if we had a user overridden primary color. That meant that if the user did not change it, we would stay with the default white on blue color for the header (since that is using `text-primary-color`). However those default color values do not follow the contrast value 6. To keep the look consistent with how it was before for the default theme I adjusted to 7. That however could mean that for users that manually specified a color for the default theme right at the cutoff point between contrast 6 and 7, their header color might change.

Alternative is to add a guard around  `text-primary-color` and related keys again to only execute it if the user overrides the colors in the picker. However, even only moving the default color by a single RGB value (smallest possible change), means that then this contrast logic takes effect and switches from white on blue to black on blue in the header, so we would only be hiding the fact that our own default colors do not conform with our defined desired contrast value.

Here we're defining an Average op as a Sum op with a post-scale factor scaled by 1 / size.  But if the user were to call `allreduce_async_` directly with `op=Average` and `postscale_factor=s` they would get a different result.

In the implementation of `_allreduce_async`, it seems that the divisor is only size when ROCm is used.  Which would seem to break `op=Average` when calling `allreduce_async_` directly.  Maybe we can pull this logic into `_allreduce_async` to simplify things?

Since values are powers of two you could potentially define those too, such as

```yaml
      flags_definition:
        snow:
          bits: [4]
          description: ...
          values:
            16: True
            0: False
```
But there isn't much value in that since our masking only supports AND and not OR, and `pixel_qa="Snow"` vs `snow=True` is same same really. If we had something like `mask_one_of(..., snow=True, water=True)` then having those defined separately would be advantageous.

yes, we could make this a generic function, but maybe we should leave the normalize_chunks out of it so that each reader can just call `normalize_chunks` with it's preferred parameters (position of "auto", dtype). So this function would just be actually `get_limit_from_pytroll_chunk_size` or something, that can be removed when we deprecated the environment variable.

`normalize_chunks` does a good size at keeping the user preferences and splitting things evenly, for example if we think of AHI:
```
In [5]: da.core.normalize_chunks((2200, "auto"), shape=(22000, 22000), dtype='float')
Out[5]: 
((2200, 2200, 2200, 2200, 2200, 2200, 2200, 2200, 2200, 2200),
 (5500, 5500, 5500, 5500))
```

So, in summary, I think we should leave `normalize_chunks` to the individual readers, and have them call this function for getting the limit.

> The actual ragged array construction still occurs, with the default np.array(...) constructor (it doesn't go into the except case since a ValueError is not thrown) 

I'm confused. Wouldn't a `ValueError` still be thrown if there's a broadcast error? And how would it go through the `_create_strict_ragged_ndarray` code path if the error message changed?

Like, if I'm using NumPy 1.23 and call
```
>>> _create_possibly_ragged_tensor([np.zeros([3, 32, 32]), np.zeros([3, 64, 64])])
```

Then `_create_possibly_ragged_tensor` would call

```
np.array([np.zeros([3, 32, 32]), np.zeros([3, 64, 64])], copy=False)
```

And then `numpy` would raise:

```
ValueError: could not broadcast input array from shape (3,32,32) into shape (3,)
```

Since `"The requested array has an inhomogeneous shape" ` isn't in this error message, we would re-raise the erorr with `raise e from None` instead of falling back to `_create_strict_ragged_ndarray`.

e.g.
```
>>> os.path.join('/test', os.sep)
'/'
>>> os.path.join('/test', '/test' + os.sep)
'/test/'
>>> os.path.join('/test/', '/test/' + os.sep)
'/test//'
>>> "/test" + os.sep
'/test/'
>>> "/test/" + os.sep
'/test//'

IIRC there was a conversation in Slack months ago in which it was discussed to have some sort of `download_file` target type that could be used to download binaries from the Internet, sort of like `http_archive` is used in Bazel.

Having a target like I can imagine a BUILD file looking like:

```python
download_file(name="vals_bin", url="...", sha="...")

experimental_run_shell_command(name="vals_cmd", command="vals eval -f -", dependencies=[":vals_bin"])

helm_deployment(post_processors=[":vals_cmd"])
```

So the Helm backend wouldn't require any changes with current implementation to use that tool, if the `download_file` target was available.

The idea of the custom `helm_post_renderer` target would be so we can have additional info about the nature of the post-renderer tool. Like in here:

```python
pex_binary(name="custom")
experimental_run_shell_command(name="vals_cmd", command="vals eval -f -")

helm_post_renderer(name="custom_renderer", dependencies=[": custom"], cacheable=True)
helm_post_renderer(name="vals_renderer", dependencies=[":vals_cmd"], cacheable=False)

helm_deployment(post_processors=[":custom_renderer", ":vals_renderer"])
```

As you can see, this quickly creates an explosion of targets for just having an additional field, hence why I'm pondering this VS having a `cacheable` field in _runnable targets_ like `pex_binary`, `go_binary` or `experimental_run_shell_command`. This also doesn't feel right as that field would have any meaning in all the other use cases for those targets.

> When will otPlatDsoAccept be called? Will it be called when the TCP connection is accepted? Or will it be called when the DSO session is established?

`otPlatDsoAccept()` is a callback from DSO transport platform layer. 
- DSO transport layer uses this callback to notify the DSO core module about a a connection request from a peer and get informed whether or not the DSO module wants to accept and use this connection _for DSO operation_.
- It is not tied to DSO session establishment. DSO core module will take care of DSO session establishment. 
- It is tied to the notion of connection request at DSO transport layer (e.g., TCP connection request for DNS-over-TCP/TLS).
- If `otPlatDsoAccept()` rejects a connection request, it just means that DSO use-case does not want this connection for DSO use. Transport/platform layer can still continue to establish connection and use the connection/socket for other purposes (say DNS-over TCP). 

> IIUC, A DNS over TCP connection can be non-DSO connection for a period of time, transmitting normal DNS over TCP messages, until the client choose to establish the DSO session.

That's possible and supported in the current code. We can establish a connection with a peer and not start with DSO session establishment on it for a while. It is up to the user of DSO module (next layer above DSO, e.g. SRPL) when/how to start the DSO session establishment.

It means all arguments after the `*` are keyword _only_ arguments: https://python-3-for-scientists.readthedocs.io/en/latest/python3_advanced.html (or https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-3102/ if you really want the hard details :smile: ).

Advantages of using it for functions with long argument lists include:
- no chance of accidentally calling the function with positional arguments in the wrong order, you _must_ pass them as keywords
- easier to refactor the function signature in future, if you want to insert a new argument or reorder the existing ones for legibility you can do so without a breaking change since the position of the arguments doesn't matter.

I'm not crazy about trying to return a "friendly" 500 error here - in general, we shouldn't ever really *intend* to return a 500 error, because a 500 should mean "the server encountered an unexpected failure condition" which typically isn't something a user would be expected to debug themselves. So returning a 500 deliberately for a particular case is kind of an antipattern to me.

IMO, the simplest resolution here is to fix the one known case (`b"{}"`) that is specific to the text plugin, and otherwise leave the behavior unchanged.

I agree it would be nice to avoid the 500 entirely when encountering invalid plugin metadata for the sake of robustness (although I don't think it's a particularly common issue).  If we do that, all plugins have basically the same codepath and ideally we'd fix them all, to avoid having the codepaths diverge. We can avoid the 500 by logging a warning and then just skipping this tag - we have similar logic already that does this if the metadata has a version field that's too new: https://cs.opensource.google/tensorflow/tensorboard/+/master:tensorboard/plugins/text/text_plugin.py;l=245-247;drc=519c6bb340294a45e17fad0353bdb5620b61abed  So we'd need to change parse_plugin_metadata() to do something like return None if it can't be parsed, and then also skip the tag in that case. (We might be able to update MetadataVersionChecker to handle this, to avoid needing the same boilerplate in every plugin.)

In terms of making 500s easier to debug in general, our current logic for turning exceptions into HTTP error responses only shows the exception error message if they are subclasses of our PublicError class: https://cs.opensource.google/tensorflow/tensorboard/+/master:tensorboard/backend/application.py;l=565-582;drc=e23de9dfca79048b570389c5b025ad8788197d7a This is for the reasons Adrian mentioned - we have to be a bit cautious not to disclose details in errors that weren't intended to be public (more of a concern for hosted services, but they also use this logic right now).  I also agree it'd be useful if we could lift that restriction for ourselves, but it'd be most effective to do that by adjusting this exception handler so it applies to all exceptions, not just specific known cases.  For example, we could consider customizing the handler so that we show more detail if the user is a TB developer, or if the binary is run with some kind of "dev mode" startup flag or environment variable (but we'd have to figure out what signals to use for those cases).

@AaronFriel It's not a no-op. It's used [here](https://github.com/pulumi/pulumi/pull/10831/files#diff-f7917202f932e91c5a03a811977f165efebbc5c006159c0a8865e3a93713048cR1655).

TL;DR: The mangling will be removed in https://github.com/pulumi/pulumi/issues/8613 and having absolute paths makes that much easier IMO.

> As we're generating all of these files, we shouldn't need path mangling: we can generate correct paths, rather than relying on aliasing/rewrite rules in tsconfig.

I assume you're referring to [this snippet](https://github.com/pulumi/pulumi/pull/10831/files#diff-f7917202f932e91c5a03a811977f165efebbc5c006159c0a8865e3a93713048cR1647-R1659), which translates paths relative to `/types` to a top-level path.

You're right, it is technically possible to track the correct relative path and use that instead. I would have used that approach but it became probability invasive. Here's why:

I found that the existing logic for generating relative paths incorrect. `mod.getRelativePath()` operates using the `mod.mod` field, which is always a single path, so the function will always return `..` no matter what mod it's operating on. Further, I had a _rough_ time adapting the logic to the nested types directories. Many functions assume that the code generated is at most two levels removed from the parent at any time, which is not true after this PR. Lots of high level functions call `getRelativePath` deeply in their callstack.
One workaround is to adopt a recursive approach where we carry the context of how deep we are between function calls. In general, this would need to adjust some number of functions to accept a `parentDir` parameter to track how deep we are in the file hierarchy (or, since we know the depth of the filesystem is <= 2, do something less DRY). This parameter needs to be threaded to a number of existing methods that don't already have it, and any calls to those methods need to supply a default value of some kind. 

I actually implemented this on `intoIOFiles` before realizing I could use `paths` (it's still in there now). The knock-on effects of threading this through `intoIOFiles`:

1. Must add the parameter to `genOwnedTypes`.
2. ...which means I have to add the parameter to `sdk.ImportsWithPath`
3. ...which means threading it through `getRelativePath` because the original logic was no longer valid. 
4. Same with `genNestedTypes` because it is corecursive with `intoIOFiles`.
5. Same with `genIndexFile`

I think, ultimately, the architecture using an absolute path will look similar to what you have in mind here. As part of https://github.com/pulumi/pulumi/issues/8613 we need to build a map between type decls and their defn dependencies. If we use relative paths, then we have to recompute the map for each subdirectory. If we use absolute paths, we only need to compute it once (or once per mod context, depending on which end of the elephant you stare at).

I actually think mapping the package root to `@` via `paths` can dramatically simplify our code, both by letting us refactor existing code and also making future changes easier.
For existing code, this lets us rip out `getRelativePath`, which as I mentioned is vacuously correct. For future code, it enabled a cleaner approach to implementing https://github.com/pulumi/pulumi/issues/8613.

Aside: I also think for most of our imports abs paths will be easier to read in the generated code than relative paths. Most of the imports are `@/s3` or `@/ec2`. With relative paths, you can have both `../../s3` and `../s3`, and they point to the same file.

How about this: We treat them the same, i.e. whatever is passed to `num_boost_rounds` is how many _final_ iterations the model should have been trained for.

E.g. when a user passes a model that has been trained for 20 iterations, with num_boost_round=30, it is trained for 10 more iterations and then stops.

We make this very clear in the docstring. We can also log an info message if we adjusted the num boost rounds to make it even clearer. We throw an error if the num_boost_rounds is smaller than what the model has already been trained for.

Then we don't have to maintain multiple complex paths. If users want to continue training an existing model for n more rounds, they just have to adjust n themselves.

Just to clarify what's happening, two consecutive changes were made here:
1) We no longer have the `to_outgoing_amqp_message` on the AmqpAnnotatedMessage, b/c we want to avoid adding pyamqp/uamqp specific code in AmqpAnnotatedMessage. I was also a little surprised to see that the EC2 client in question wasn't stubbed by the existing `ec2_client_stub` pytest fixture. It turns out that the `@lru_cache` decorator treats explicit specification of an optional default value differently than implicit specification of the same value. In this case, given a function definition like:
 ```python
 @lru_cache
 def resource_cache(name, region, max_retries=BOTO_MAX_RETRIES, **kwargs)
 ```
Calling `resource_cache("ec2", "us-west-2", BOTO_MAX_RETRIES)` resolves to a different cache key than `resource_cache("ec2", "us-west-2")`, even though `max_retries` equals `BOTO_MAX_RETRIES` in both cases. This is discussed a bit more over at: https://stackoverflow.com/questions/36771866/memoization-caching-with-default-optional-arguments.

The above detour, while interesting, made me think that it may be a good idea to open up a separate PR introducing a README to the AWS test directory discussing best practices for writing and running stubbed AWS unit tests. 🙂
